mnemonics: A POSet Has Order, But Not Always A Complete Chain: Think of a family tree (reflexive, antisymmetric, transitive) where not everyone is directly comparable.
keyPoints: A partially ordered set (poset) is a set equipped with a binary relation that is reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive.
Not every pair of elements in a poset needs to be comparable (e.g., one is not necessarily greater than or less than the other).
Common examples include the set of subsets of a given set ordered by inclusion (⊆) and the set of natural numbers ordered by divisibility (|).
commonMistakes: Assuming all elements in a poset must be comparable to each other. This is only true for a totally ordered set, which is a special type of poset.
Confusing antisymmetry (if a ≤ b and b ≤ a, then a = b) with asymmetry (if a ≤ b, then b ≰ a). Asymmetry is a stronger condition.
